content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for movies that make you appreciate life and/or being alive

**Score:** 50
**Body:** Like the title says, I’m looking for movies that make you appreciate life and/or being alive. They can be from any time period and in any language. I’m very grateful to anyone who recommends a movie, so thank you. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's a Wonderful Life
2. perhaps not the angle you were going for, but The Truman Show
  - Solid suggestion 👌 
3. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
4. Big Fish.  It's truly one of the best movies ever made.
5. A Man Called Otto (or Ove)

Little miss Sunshine
6. Forrest Gump - "Life is like a box of chocolates. You never know what you're gonna get!"
7. Paddington! I know it sounds a bit silly, but there's nothing like a lovable bear to really make you appreciate. Enjoy and all the best!
8. Wings of Desire dir. Wim Wenders

existentialism philosophy
9. Perfect Days

## 2. Movies with impressive sound design

**Score:** 21
**Body:** This means not only action movies where the ears are pleased with explosions and other things. But also any other movies where the sound design is something special. When you really notice it

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. David Lynch's work is what you're looking for. His first film Eraserhead and his later works like Lost Highway and Inland Empire in particular.
2. The Zone of interest comes to mind. Horrifying movie where all the action happens off screen. Your only exposure to the horror is the background noise.
3. Arrival is a big one. Half the tension comes from the sound itself, not what you’re seeing. Same with No Country for Old Men, the quiet feels louder than explosions. Annihilation too, that bear scene lives rent free in my ears. Sound design doing psychological damage in the best way.
4. the conversation
5. The Tale of the Princess Kaguya (2013) is truly beautiful
6. Altman, with his overlapping dialogue and “natural” soundscapes, is always good for this. My favorite is The Long Goodbye, but for sound design I’d suggest Nashville.
7. Blink Twice
8. I recently watched Everest(2015)if you like wind and winter sound this movie doesn’t let up with that
9. Some of my favorites are WALL-E, War of the Worlds (2005 obv), and Dune
10. Star Wars

## 3. Need Recommendations: Movies That Mess With Reality

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I just rewatched Inception and realized I love movies that play with time, dreams, or alternate realities. Stuff that really challenges your perception.
What other films make you think for days afterward? I already know Interstellar and Shutter Island, so looking for something less obvious too.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. i would say Arrival
2. Donnie Darko(2001)
3. The Game (1997)
A wealthy, cold banker (Michael Douglas) receives a mysterious birthday gift from his estranged brother(Sean Penn). The gift; a chance to play a personalized, real-life game run by Consumer Recreation Services (CRS). The game blurs the lines between reality and fiction, thrusting him into increasingly dangerous, elaborate scenarios that challenge his sanity and force him to confront his past. The game seems intent on destroying him, leaving him unable to trust anyone - all leading to a shocking reveal about the game's true purpose. 

The Thirteenth Floor (1999)
A sci-fi thriller (set in present day 1990s) where computer scientist Douglas Hall investigates the murder of his mentor, finding clues within a hyper-realisticvirtual reality simulation of 1937 Los Angeles. As Douglas delves deeper, exploring the simulation as his avatar "John Ferguson," he uncovers shocking truths about his own reality, discovering that his world might not be what it seems. His investigation leads to existential questions about consciousness and reality. The film blends virtual reality concepts with film noir mystery, exploring themes of free will and simulated worlds. It’s was released around the same time of the beautiful, action filled ‘The Matrix’ and got list in that movies shadow. 
4. 12 monkeys
5. Try Coherence, Primer, Triangle, The Prestige, Enemy, Dark City, and Timecrimes.
6. Someone else mentioned Primer, yes that is good. Others:

13th Floor

12 Monkeys

Dark City

The Matrix

Total Recall (The old one)

Identity 

Edge of Tomorrow 

The Truman Show

Sphere

Groundhog Day

Looper

Moon

Run Lola Run

The Sixth Sense

Event Horizon (Horror)


Not quite what you asking for but close:

Ghost in the Shell

The Time Machine

Pandorum (Horror)

Ex Machina

Blade Runner
7. everything everywhere all at once
8. Enter the Void
9. It's What's Inside (2024)
10. *eXistenZ* (1999)

## 4. Are there any scary movie suggestions like 30 days a night?

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I’m trying to find a good movie in a desolate cold setting. Like a camping, Alaska, what have you. Anybody got anything that is similar to 30 days a night? I’ve already watched that a million times I feel.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The thing
2. Misery
3. Let the right one in 


The grey- More of a thriller but it’s in Alaska and you have wolves instead of vamps
4. Antlers

Season 4 of True Detective
5. *The Fourth Kind* (2009) is set in Alaska. I found it to be an excellent horror film.
6. The Terror (TV series)

The Ritual
7. Insomnia (2002) -- thriller with Robin Williams and Al Pacino. Very good! Not scary, but tense.
8. The Mist
9. Frozen (2010) - skiers stranded on a chairlift
10. Crimson Peak (2015) - remote mansion, snow, supernatural happenings, a Guillermo Del Toro + Legendary Pictures film.

## 5. Looking for movies or series where the protagonist is on the run for his life and also solving the mystery/conspiracy.

**Score:** 17
**Body:** As per the title, please suggest me movies / tv series where the protagonist is on the run for his life and also solving the mystery/conspiracy. 

Obvious example is The Fugitive.

Many thanks.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There's a name for this motif that you are looking for. Called the "wrong man", it was a favorite of Hitchcock, and can be seen in many of his films, including *Young & Innocent, The 39 Steps*, *North by Northwest, I Confess,* *Saboteur, To Catch a Thief*. and *The Wrong Man*.
2. Enemy of the State (1998)
3. The Game
  - beat me to it
4. Three Days of the Condor (1975) 
5. Minority Report (2002)
6. The Net 

Reacher 

The Firm

Anti-Trust

The Night Agent

Double Jeopardy 

Enemy of the State

The Bourne Identity
7. The Bourne movies are perfect for this - dude's constantly running while piecing together his own identity and the whole conspiracy behind it. Also check out Enemy of the State with Will Smith, that one's a wild ride
8. Just had a flashback to an old mini series called ‘See How They Run’. Loved it growing up
9. You should definitely check out the Bourne identity. It is the gold standard for this trope. Watching him piece together his identity while escaping assassins is just as thrilling as The Fugitive.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. How much would you rate this movie?

**Score:** 1132

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Brilliant. Pike is fucking terrifying
  - I Care A Lot fills me with rage.

She's so psychotic and *believable*. That's the sticking point.
    - I love I Care A Lot. Her performance made the whole story seem believable and enraging
2. Incredibly rewatchable, peak Fincher, with a stellar story and cast. I loved it.
  - Hard agree, despite knowing how it ends I'm always happy to re watch it. Its up there with 7 and fight club
3. A riveting thriller, 8.5/10. Ben Affleck is convincing. Rosamund Pike acted the psycho role w/ perfection...
4. I really enjoy movies that successfully fuck with you.

This one fucked with me.
5. 10/10
6. 8/10

I like the mood. Rosamunde Pike is unlikeable but that makes a good charakter writing.
  - I read the book in 1 day. Pike and Affleck were perfectly cast.

## 2. An actor you simply can’t imagine in normal roles because of that one particular role he played just too damn well. My pick is Antony starr

**Score:** 446
**Body:** I simply can’t see him doing anything usual or normal in films.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I would agree with you about Starr, except I watched him in Banshee too, and he was great in it.
  - I mean, in that show he is just as crazy if not worse xD
2. Michael Scott did play a great Steve Carell
  - Oh how the turn tables
3. Glen Howerton
  - He was great in Blackberry but I still couldn't get away from thinking it was Dennis on some side quest or parallel universe
  - A golden God is a golden God.
4. Aaron Paul YO 
5. I saw him in Banshee first so I struggle to buy him as homelander sometimes
6. The best example is Alfonso Ribeiro.


He was to damn good as Carlton. Can only see him as Carlton. What a double edged sword

## 3. Watching district 9 a movie that absolutely slaps! The Mobile suit scene chef's kiss

**Score:** 272

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Literally just finished it 2 hours ago. Haven’t seen it in years, holy crap this movie hits.
2. Such a good movie. Great ending.
  - I never finished this movie. I should go back and watch it in full.


I was watching it at a theater I worked at as one of the more senior/training projectionists. I was off duty, but it had a malfunction (brain wrap) causing the film to stop and burn through. I ran up to the booth out of instinct and spent like 2 hours repairing the print. My manager ended up clocking me in for the time spent, lol.
3. Can you believe he had sex with aliens though? Pervert.
  - There was a bukkake.
4. I went into the theater with like 0 expectations because I think all I knew of it was this poster and I was like 15. Completely blown away.
5. Fookin Prauwns!
6. i can’t wait for the sequel
  - That shit is never gonna happen. District 10 has been a fantasy since the movie came out, sadly. Seriously would’ve been amazing but District 9 really does capture lightning in a bottle.
7. I told my wife about this movie. I told her it’s about aliens, kinda. She kind of rolled her eyes and gave a *blecckk* sound. Understandably, it’s a genre that’s just not her thing. So I saw it featured on hbo or something months later. I put it on randomly, and she asked like 10min in what it was about. I said it was that “alien type” movie and she was soooo not down. So I told her it’s really not like that, and she gave it a chance. It’s one of her favorite movies now. Incredible movie.

## 4. what other movies have as much product placement as The Truman Show?

**Score:** 137

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’m worried you think it was real product placement and not satire.
  - I respect Wayne's World for managing to be both.
    - I can't talk about it any more. It's giving me a headache.
      - Here. Take two of these.
        - It’s like, people only do things because they get paid, and that’s really sad
2. Talladega Nights: The Ballad of Ricky Bobby
  - If you don’t chew Big Red then fuck you.

https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=ONtKjljX8KI
3. Wayne’s World features my favourite satirical take on product placement, but movies like *I, Robot* and *Man of Steel* surely have to take the cake in terms of mainstream films. Then there’s *The Greatest Movie Ever Sold*, which deliberately sets out to be entirely funded by sponsors.
  - It's like people only do things because they get paid, and that's just really sad.

*Reebok*
4. Josie and the Pussycats has another great meta use of product placement.  

## 5. Tonight’s watch, what’s your opinion?

**Score:** 108

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This one gots the juice!

Becky is like a superhero but her power is that she’s a super-angry middle school girl.

Kevin James? Totally legit bad guy.
  - Yes she is!! && I enjoyed watching him as the bad guy for once
    - He was a great bad guy, and I love how she shows no restraint. I see a lot of daughter in Becky.

Hoping we get >!a third and find out about the key!<.
2. I love this movie! Highly recommend. The 2nd one is also good but not as good as the first (as is tradition). I also think there is going to be a 3rd 🤔
  - Was better than I expected, oh I wasn’t aware there was a second I’ll definitely check it out thank you!
3. Kevin James' intimidation factor drops off fast, maybe too fast. He has his moments don't get me wrong, and he cuts a striking figure in this especially with the massive swastika tattoo, but eventually you realize he is Kevin James, and it kills it a bit for me. He is dangerously one note in his delivery through this.  

I highly recommend the sequel. Sean William Scott is proving himself to be a great late career bloomer with recent roles, particularly since Goon, and he plays a equally supremacist villain in the sequel, but is able to deliver a bit more on the intimidation factor. There is also a lot more satire in the 2nd one, that while being pretty on the nose is still very fun.
  - He’s not made to play a bad guy role, (the tattoos made me chuckle) explained why he threw out that line in German ….but I enjoyed seeing him in a different light.. thanks I’m definitely going to watch the second now!
4. Thumbs up!
  - Really? It’s been sitting in my queue for a long time. I’ll check it out. Saw there was a sequel, too. 🚀
5. Sequel is great too!

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Great cinematography in sub-$2-3M films from the last 5 years?

**Score:** 51
**Body:** I’m looking for recs for films from the last 5 years that had great cinematography on a tight budget. I’ve found you all are great at spotting underrated gems and debating why they work.

By low budget, I mean under $2-3M (or even less). Especially interested in films that have a strong visual language through restraint rather than scale or vfx.

Some films I love are *Before Sunrise*, *Aftersun*, *Columbus*, and *Past Lives* (I know this had a bigger budget, but had the right tone). I'm interested in more intimate films with solid composition, light and camera movement that complement a strong story.

I’m a filmmaker prepping a small indie project (under 1M) this spring and want to make sure I’m not missing the work of a talented cinematographer may have flown under the radar.

Thanks!

EDIT: Some GREAT recs here. I've added a bunch of films to my watchlist. I'd love to hear more US based films due to my DP search. Thanks! 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Drive My Car (2021) was made on a 1.3M budget. The cinematography is calm and restrained, relying on static compositions and long takes to let emotions unfold naturally. Natural lighting and balanced framing emphasize silence, distance, and introspection rather than visual spectacle. It's a rather lovely film.
  - While this is often true the film has a few notable moments of more brazen camera work (the cigarettes in the car) that are really important as a contrast to much of the movie.
  - Had no idea that was the budget. They did an amazing job, especially with the number of scenes in film and stretching the budget. 

While I appreciated the film immensely, looking for US based films since that's going to be shot in Michigan.
2. Universal Language (2024)

Good One (2024)

**Evil Does Not Exist (2023)**

Janet Planet (2023)

Fallen Leaves (2023)

Showing Up (2022)

How to Blow up a Pipeline (2022)

**Memoria (2022)**

Petite Maman (2021)

Edit: bolded what I think are the best of the best in this category, but everything here definitely fits your criteria. I love your acknowledgement that cinematography isn't vfx or scale but what you are able to accomplish with use of camera.
  - Thanks! Will def check these out. Good One was great. Solid turn and story and a small scale. Haven’t seen the others!
3. *The Vourdulak* (2023). No budget figures I can find, but it’s French and was shot on 16mm with practical effects. 

It looks exactly like those old 70’s euro-horror films. It’s so cool.
4. A little bit older but The Vast of Night (2017) is a perfect fit. 

There’s a very technical one shot going through the whole town with no edits. My only qualm is that the dialogue mixing is a little muddy for how quick talking it is. Really carried by great acting. 

All for $700,000.
5. Not sure of the budget (probably not much more than your limit), but I really enjoyed the cinematography of “It Was Just An Accident”, an excellent film from Iranian director Jafar Panahi which won the Palme d’Or this year. There are two incredible long-take scenes which mirror each other, one tracking in wide angle and one shot in extreme close-up at night. The movie is also shot in a way to intentionally omit details we know are there from some shots, both thematically relevant and used to put us off-kilter. I won’t say more, it’s a fantastic movie and one you’re better off not knowing much about going in.
6. - The Wig

- The Looming Storm

- The Hater

- Red Rooms

All superb in their own right. And not just for low-budget films — they feature some of the finest cinematography of the past half-decade period, despite their limited funding
7. Not very under the radar, but Sean Baker's Red Rocket looked pretty great on a budget of ~$1m. I don't know much about technical camera stuff, but I guess it was shot all on 16mm, which might be grainier than some people like, but I thought it was beautiful.

## 2. Explain the ending of In Bruges

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Spoilers below, obviously

Fiennes shoots Farrell with one of the dum-dum rounds he acquired earlier. This is necessary for the plot, because it obliterates the dwarf's face, so that Fiennes doesn't realize he didn't actually kill a child. (Let's assume for the sake of argument that the bullet would actually have such a drastic effect.) Yet it's also necessary that the bullet goes through Farrell, so that Fiennes doesn't actually realize there's someone else in the line of fire (mirroring Farrell's own mistake at the beginning).

So it seems for the purpose of the plot, the bullet leaves the barrel as full metal jacket, goes straight through Farrell as such bullets will do, leaving him alive to utter the final voiceover, then appears to experience a sudden change of heart, transforms into a dum-dum bullet, and blows off the dwarf's head. Am I misunderstanding something here?

Is the answer basically that a wizard did it?

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. So, lemme get in the bag for two seconds. Dum-dums is an old school nickname for hollow tips and soft point rounds. Hollow tips expand on impact. Helps prevent over penetration to a point.

Ray got hit with several. One went stray and hit lil dude with obvious results. It's a really simple twist on the events from the beginning. A book end in three ways.

Both characters get sloppy and wind up hitting a bystander. The antagonist lives up to his personal code. Ray reminds us he always hated Bruges, so it's upsetting he might die there.
  - Yeah as far as guns go there's basically no mystery here. Sometimes hollow point rounds go through people. People aren't ballistic gel. 

OP there's just nothing here to talk about. And I dare say worrying about the past some bullet took is not really in the spirit of this sub. I suspect this will get deleted by the mods.
    - I really don't hate the question. Because it definitely lives up to the mystical side of the story. But like you mention, the scenario is played so bluntly that we both (and plenty of other viewers) had the same reaction.


OP might not have known that, and that's ok. It's a bit fun to have that reaction come out of someone.
  - I agree to a point but it's important for the plot that Fiennes shoot *through* Farrell in order not to realize whom he hit (and mirror the event that caused him to shoot Farrell in the first place). In the scene he clearly does shoot through Farrell. This presents a clear problem, since it requires a bullet that itself comprehends the idea of poetic justice.
    - No, that's just over penetration lol It's why they tell people 'always make note of what's behind what you're aiming at'.


You could almost add a title card, 'Exhibit A'.
      - >that's just over penetration

The technical problem here is that a hollow point (dum dum, in common parlance) bullet is specifically designed not to overpenetrate like an FMJ bullet. It breaks up on impact. It's not going to go straight through a body, remain intact, then break up when it hits the next target. One can ascribe this behavior to the poetry of the story, which seems guided by the ideas of karma and spiritual redemption, but then there's the problem of the fact that that karma was served up only at the expense of an innocent bystander's getting shot in the face.
2. The honest answer is your enjoyment of some films will depend on if you're on board with some amount of plot contrivance. 

Usually our suspension of disbelief is greater when it concerns dramatic irony, because we find the contrivance 'fitting' in the story. 

Especially in the religious context of In Bruges, where the bullet going through might be seen as a divine retribution or karma. The film talks about revenge and punishment, and it's message seems to imply that everyone gets what's coming to them through their own actions (a sort of self-fulfilling prophecy).

So, as long as we accept these bullets might only explode when, for example, coming to a halt or after a certain time, we can accept that it just turned out that way because it is befitting of our story.
  - It's interesting that on the one hand it's poetic justice (someone redeemable is saved, whereas the irredeemable Fiennes gets his comeuppance). Yet on the other hand, the sacrificial agent of this justice is an innocent bystander. The dwarf did nothing to what he got.
    - >The dwarf did nothing to what he got.

He wasn't reaallly shit. But he wasn't all that great either... A bit like Tottenham
  - >and it's message seems to imply that everyone gets what's coming to them through their own actions (a sort of self-fulfilling prophecy).

Except for children and little people, I guess

## 3. how do you feel about stuff like Quentin Tarantino putting fetish stuff in his movies? do you think it's exploitative?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** personally i think it's kind of gross, not just from the perspective of someone putting their weird fetish in a movie but making other people participate in it. from dusk till dawn comes to mind as a particularly egregious example. i'm sure the actress in the bar was probably fine with it but what if someone wasn't super cool with it but they still wanted to be in a movie, the power dynamic feels at least mildly coercive especially with him casting himself in that role to have a hot woman he hired to play an acting role put her feet in his mouth so he could personally get off to it.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don’t want to be unduly mean or aggressive but this kind of comment feels like it belongs to a family of comments that is using a fairly boring and shallow application of power dynamics and therapy speak to neuter and defang interesting art. Like ok Tarantino has a foot fetish. It’s one of like a gazillion little impulses or ideas or preferences that makes their way into his stuff. You don’t have to like all of them or share them but why should he have to censor his impulses because looking at feet in a sexualized way may feel yucky to you? All of those impulses and preferences added together make up his unique voice/vision/style and we need more of that in film not less. And what’s the difference between a fetish and, like, a sort of style preference (like a love of extreme facial closeups or a tendency to cast similar looking leads). Anyways. Let’s all try to stop inventing harm where there isn’t any. 
2. I know his homage to Russ Myer, who would pack his movies with large breasts. It is what it is, and with Tarantino, he made a career off taking 70s exploitation tropes and turning them into critic pleasing movies. As long as this doesn't lead to something weird, like a next generation director that's obsessed with putting scrotums or the taint in all of his movies.
3. I dont mean to be pedantic but you chose a movie that wasnt actually directed by Tarantino and I feel like there are far more egregious examples than him. I get your point though but the way you framed it seems like you're gunning for him specifically. 

To really answer your question I think it depends on the movie and is a case by case basis. Lars Van Trier's Antichrist goes farther than anything Tarantino's done. It also sounds like the horror stories that have come out of David O Russell's sets are worse too. Quentin isn't a saint though but I dont think he's the worst offender out there.
4. Robert Rodriguez directed From Dusk Till Dawn. Athough it's QT's screenplay and they're BFFs, but the example doesn't support your argument as presented. 

Anyway, I see the feet thing mostly as one of his maker's marks that's benign and at some point probably turned into an obligation because it became a thing with his movies. Like the overhead spotlight, music, and whatever else he does in most all his movies that make them his movies.
5. Do I think people putting sex and sex related stuff, even kinky stuff at that, into movies is inherently exploitative? Not in the sense that you appear to be thinking about it at least. 

Is there always a potential for power imbalances in a director/actor working relationship that can lead to exploitation on- and off-set? Certainly. But this does not inherently have something to do with the subject matter or sex-related things depicted in the movie.
6. Have you watched Cronenberg, by the way? *Dead Ringers* is practically saturated with fetish. Every time you think he’s reached the limit, he adds another layer and somehow makes it work. He doesn’t even need nudity. That’s kind of the point, I guess. If you know what you’re looking at, the hint is enough.
7. I mean compared to a lot of famous people in recent years (I'm thinking Diddy, Epstein or Weinstein) etc. I think Tarantino is pretty tame since he just puts unnecessary shots of the actress's feet in his movies. Yeah there's the one movie where he drinks the tequila from the actress's foot but I'm sure she agreed to the role.
8. The love of violence, gore, an torture are also fetishes. They are just so common in our films that we don’t think about them that way. 

The history of nudity in film involves a lot of coercion, and I think—compared to the exploitation flicks that inspired him—Tarantino’s work is fairly tame. For all the possible ways women’s bodies have been exploited on film, I think the foot stuff is fairly tame. 

I don’t know of any instance of an actress complaining about the foot thing, though it’s certainly possible. And I can certainly understand it making someone uncomfortable. 

The conversation reminds me of what Samuel Jackson said when asked about Tarantino’s use of the N word. To summarize, he doesn’t like the way Tarantino uses the word necessarily, but he’s gotten to play a wider variety of big parts working with Tarantino than other actors get to play in their lifetimes. 

That doesn’t make coercing people acceptable, but Sam Jackson would rather work with Tarantino than not, because nobody else gives him (or black actors in general) such a wide variety of big roles to play.
9. He makes the closest thing to white exploitation movies I can think of. He makes pulpy b movie style movies. Outside of Django and that doesn't have much of that stuff in it 

I think exploitation is kinda ingrained the kind of movies hes harking to.

---
